radiology_order.exam: LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN) PORT
BILAT UP EXT VEINS US PORT

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone with biliary obstruction; abdominal compartment syndrome (intra-abdominal hypertension from massive fluid resuscitation); peripancreatic fluid collection or pseudocyst
ensemble.information_gap: No cross-sectional or repeat abdominal imaging has been performed at this institution — the only prior imaging was an outside RUQ ultrasound that raised concern for a CBD stone, and no CT has been done anywhere; the physician needs to characterize the biliary tree (CBD dilation, stone), assess the pancreas for necrosis or fluid collections, and evaluate for intra-abdominal pathology contributing to the ventilatory deterioration and oliguria (e.g., massive ascites, abdominal compartment syndrome).
ensemble.expected_finding: Dilated common bile duct (>6–7 mm) with or without a visible choledocholithiasis, pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening suggesting cholecystitis, peripancreatic fluid or edema, and/or large-volume ascites that could explain rising plateau pressures and oliguria.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has severe acute pancreatitis with a suspected CBD stone on outside RUQ US, no CT has been performed, ERCP was aborted without cannulation, and he is now critically ill with worsening ventilation and oliguria after 13 L of resuscitation — a bedside abdominal ultrasound is the fastest, safest first-line imaging to re-evaluate the biliary tree and detect ascites or other new abdominal pathology without the risks of transport for CT.
grounding: History: 'lipase 13,500, elevated LFT, and RUQ US w concern for possible CBD stone'
History: 'CT scan was not performed at OSH or at ___ thus far'
History: 'severe duodenal edema which distorted the major papilla, so the procedure was aborted without cannulation'
History: 'UOP dropped to <10cc/hr and has become increasingly difficult to ventilate, with plateau pressures in the high ___ and peak pressures in the upper ___'
History: 'He has received approximately 13L fluid over the past 24hours'
Labs: 'Lipase: 1847.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)'
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Direct: 0.6 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)'; 'Bilirubin, Total: 1.9 mg/dL'
Labs: 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)'
Labs: 'Anion Gap: 25.0 mEq/L (ref 8.0-20.0)'
ensemble.reasoning: This critically ill patient has severe acute pancreatitis (lipase 13,500 at OSH, still 1847 here) with an outside RUQ ultrasound raising concern for a CBD stone, elevated direct bilirubin and AST, and a failed ERCP — yet no CT or repeat biliary imaging has been obtained. He is now intubated with worsening plateau pressures and near-anuric oliguria after massive fluid resuscitation, raising concern for abdominal compartment syndrome, large ascites, or an evolving biliary/pancreatic complication. A bedside liver/gallbladder ultrasound is the most immediately accessible, radiation-free tool to reassess CBD diameter, look for gallstones or pericholecystic changes, and detect free fluid — all of which would directly guide the next intervention (repeat ERCP attempt, percutaneous drainage, or surgical decompression).

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone with biliary obstruction; abdominal compartment syndrome (intra-abdominal hypertension from massive fluid resuscitation); peripancreatic fluid collection or pseudocyst
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: No cross-sectional or repeat abdominal imaging has been performed at this institution — the only prior imaging was an outside RUQ ultrasound that raised concern for a CBD stone, and no CT has been done anywhere; the physician needs to characterize the biliary tree (CBD dilation, stone), assess the pancreas for necrosis or fluid collections, and evaluate for intra-abdominal pathology contributing to the ventilatory deterioration and oliguria (e.g., massive ascites, abdominal compartment syndrome).

representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The patient has severe acute pancreatitis with a suspected CBD stone on outside RUQ US, no CT has been performed, ERCP was aborted without cannulation, and he is now critically ill with worsening ventilation and oliguria after 13 L of resuscitation — a bedside abdominal ultrasound is the fastest, safest first-line imaging to re-evaluate the biliary tree and detect ascites or other new abdominal pathology without the risks of transport for CT.

History: 'CT scan was not performed at OSH or at ___ thus far'
History: 'severe duodenal edema which distorted the major papilla, so the procedure was aborted without cannulation'
History: 'UOP dropped to <10cc/hr and has become increasingly difficult to ventilate, with plateau pressures in the high ___ and peak pressures in the upper ___'

Labs: 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 116.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-40.0)'
Labs: 'Anion Gap: 25.0 mEq/L (ref 8.0-20.0)'
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This critically ill patient has severe acute pancreatitis (lipase 13,500 at OSH, still 1847 here) with an outside RUQ ultrasound raising concern for a CBD stone, elevated direct bilirubin and AST, and a failed ERCP — yet no CT or repeat biliary imaging has been obtained. He is now intubated with worsening plateau pressures and near-anuric oliguria after massive fluid resuscitation, raising concern for abdominal compartment syndrome, large ascites, or an evolving biliary/pancreatic complication. A bedside liver/gallbladder ultrasound is the most immediately accessible, radiation-free tool to reassess CBD diameter, look for gallstones or pericholecystic changes, and detect free fluid — all of which would directly guide the next intervention (repeat ERCP attempt, percutaneous drainage, or surgical decompression).

vindication.actual_finding: The common hepatic duct measured only 4 mm with no intrahepatic biliary dilation, no gallstones, no gallbladder wall thickening or pericholecystic fluid, and no ascites; the pancreas was not visualized due to bowel gas.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report contradicts the expected findings of CBD dilation, choledocholithiasis, cholecystitis features, and large-volume ascites, while leaving the pancreas unassessed and thus not resolving the information gap regarding peripancreatic pathology.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Abdominal compartment syndrome progression, SMV thrombus extension, pancreatic necrosis evolution, or new intra-abdominal complication (e.g., bowel ischemia, hemorrhage) in the setting of open abdomen
ensemble.information_gap: The prior CTA (with contrast) documented pancreatic necrosis at the neck/proximal body, SMV thrombus, and an open abdomen for decompression of abdominal compartment syndrome; the physician now needs to reassess the extent of pancreatic necrosis, fluid collections, and vascular complications — but the patient's deteriorating renal function (oliguria <10 cc/hr, granular casts, proteinuria 300 mg/dL) and hemodynamic instability raise concern about contrast nephrotoxicity, making a non-contrast study the safer surveillance option.
ensemble.expected_finding: Interval change in pancreatic necrosis extent, new or enlarging peripancreatic fluid collections, hemorrhage, or signs of abdominal compartment syndrome recurrence — identifiable without IV contrast given the prior contrast-enhanced baseline for comparison
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: Given documented pancreatic necrosis with SMV thrombus on prior CTA, clinical deterioration (oliguria <10 cc/hr, rising plateau pressures, 13 L fluid resuscitation), and urinalysis showing granular casts and proteinuria suggesting acute kidney injury, a non-contrast CT is appropriate to reassess intra-abdominal disease severity while avoiding additional nephrotoxic contrast exposure.
grounding: CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: 'There is acute edematous ____ with an area of necrosis in the pancreatic neck/proximal body. There is a moderate volume pancreatic ascites.'
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: 'There is a small area of thrombus in the SMV seen on series 4B image 130.'
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: 'There is a dehiscent midline abdominal wound created surgically for decompression of abdominal compartment syndrome.'
History: 'his UOP dropped to <10cc/hr and has become increasingly difficult to ventilate, with plateau pressures in the high ___ and peak pressures in the upper ___'
History: 'He has received approximately 13L fluid over the past 24hours.'
Labs: Granular Casts: 5.0 #/lpf (ref 0.0-0.0)
Labs: Protein: 300.0 mg/dL (urine)
Labs: Urine Color: RED
Labs: Phosphate: 5.7 mg/dL (ref 2.7-4.5) — hyperphosphatemia consistent with renal dysfunction despite creatinine 0.8
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has confirmed necrotizing pancreatitis with SMV thrombus and an open abdomen, and is now deteriorating with severe oliguria, worsening ventilator parameters, and urinalysis findings (granular casts, heavy proteinuria, red urine) suggesting acute kidney injury — likely from abdominal compartment syndrome and/or ATN from hypoperfusion. A non-contrast CT abdomen is ordered to reassess the extent of pancreatic necrosis, peripancreatic collections, and any new hemorrhagic or ischemic complications without the risk of further nephrotoxic IV contrast in a patient with clear signs of renal compromise. The prior contrast-enhanced study provides a baseline against which interval changes in fluid collections and necrosis can be compared even without contrast.

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: 'There is a small area of thrombus in the SMV seen on series 4B image 130.'
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: 'There is a dehiscent midline abdominal wound created surgically for decompression of abdominal compartment syndrome.'

vindication.actual_finding: The non-contrast CT showed persistent pancreatic edema with a hypodense area at the neck/proximal body (no new gas, no hemorrhage), minimal increase in peripancreatic edema, stable moderate pancreatic ascites, and an open abdomen consistent with prior decompression — without new or enlarging fluid collections or signs of abdominal compartment syndrome recurrence.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The study successfully identified interval changes (minimal increase in peripancreatic edema, stable ascites, no hemorrhage or gas in the necrotic zone) without IV contrast, directly addressing the physician's surveillance goals while avoiding contrast nephrotoxicity.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone causing biliary obstruction; pancreatic duct disruption from necrotizing pancreatitis
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is a CBD stone, biliary stricture, or pancreatic duct disruption that is driving or complicating the pancreatitis — information that ERCP was meant to provide but could not obtain due to severe duodenal edema distorting the major papilla, and that CT without contrast cannot reliably characterize
ensemble.expected_finding: A CBD stone, biliary duct dilation, or pancreatic duct leak/disruption at the level of the pancreatic neck/proximal body (site of known necrosis) that would explain the ongoing biliary obstruction and guide further management
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: ERCP was aborted without cannulation due to duodenal edema, leaving the question of choledocholithiasis and pancreatic duct integrity unanswered; MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive alternative to delineate biliary and pancreatic ductal anatomy without requiring contrast or instrumentation in a critically ill patient.
grounding: History: 'RUQ US w concern for possible CBD stone. CT scan was not performed at OSH or at ___ thus far. He was transferred to ___ for ERCP. However there was severe duodenal edema which distorted the major papilla, so the procedure was aborted without cannulation.'
Prior imaging 1 (US): 'The CHD measures 4 mm.' and 'The pancreas is not well visualized, largely obscured by overlying bowel gas.'

Prior imaging 2 (CTA chest/abdomen): 'There is acute edematous ____ with an area of necrosis in the pancreatic neck/proximal body. There is a moderate volume pancreatic ascites.' and 'There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.'

Prior imaging 3 (CT abdomen w/o contrast): 'Previously described area of necrosis in the pancreatic neck/ proximal body is better evaluated on the prior CT with contrast. There is no pancreatic ductal dilatation.'
Labs: 'Lipase: 1847.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)', 'Asparate Aminotransferase (AST): 116.0 IU/L', 'Bilirubin, Direct: 0.6 mg/dL (ref 0.0-0.3)', 'Bilirubin, Total: 1.9 mg/dL'
ensemble.reasoning: ERCP — the intended diagnostic and therapeutic procedure for the suspected CBD stone — was aborted without cannulation because of severe duodenal edema, leaving biliary and pancreatic ductal anatomy completely uncharacterized by direct visualization. The CT with contrast confirmed pancreatic necrosis at the neck/proximal body and moderate pancreatic ascites, raising concern for a main pancreatic duct disruption at that site, but CT cannot reliably depict ductal integrity or small CBD stones. MRCP is the logical next step to non-invasively map the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis and assess the pancreatic duct for disruption, both of which would directly alter management in this critically ill patient.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / common bile duct stone causing biliary obstruction; pancreatic duct disruption from necrotizing pancreatitis
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether there is a CBD stone, biliary stricture, or pancreatic duct disruption that is driving or complicating the pancreatitis — information that ERCP was meant to provide but could not obtain due to severe duodenal edema distorting the major papilla, and that CT without contrast cannot reliably characterize

vindication.actual_finding: MRCP showed no intra- or extrahepatic bile duct dilation, no ductal stones, and an intact main pancreatic duct through the region of focal necrosis at the pancreatic neck, with no evidence of pancreatic duct disruption or leak.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The physician expected to find a CBD stone, biliary dilation, or pancreatic duct leak/disruption to explain ongoing biliary obstruction, but MRCP demonstrated none of these findings — the biliary system was normal and the pancreatic duct was intact.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN) PORT)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Portal vein / SMV thrombosis progression; peripancreatic fluid collection / pseudoaneurysm development; biliary obstruction recurrence
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the peripancreatic fluid collections, portal/SMV thrombus, or vascular complications (e.g., splenic artery pseudoaneurysm) have changed since the last cross-sectional imaging, and whether the portal vein compression/attenuation seen on MRCP has progressed to frank thrombosis — all without exposing a critically ill, intubated patient to transport for CT or MRI.
ensemble.expected_finding: Change in size or character of peripancreatic fluid collections, new or evolving portal vein / SMV thrombus, gallbladder wall changes, or new ascites — assessed at bedside in an ICU-bound patient who cannot safely travel to CT/MRI.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient is critically ill and intubated in the FICU with known necrotizing pancreatitis, SMV thrombus, and peripancreatic fluid on prior imaging; a portable bedside ultrasound is the only practical modality to serially monitor vascular and fluid-collection changes without the risks of transporting an unstable, ventilator-dependent patient.
grounding: Prior imaging 2 (CTA Chest): 'There is a small area of thrombus in the SMV seen on series 4B image 130.'
Prior imaging 2 (CTA Chest): 'There is acute edematous ____ with an area of necrosis in the pancreatic neck/proximal body. There is a moderate volume pancreatic ascites.'
Prior imaging 4 (MRCP): 'There is severe attenuation of the main portal vein at the splenic and SMV confluence (series 17, image 26, 24), without definite thrombosis.'
Prior imaging 4 (MRCP): 'The peripancreatic fluid appears slightly more organized since the ___ CT, demonstrating a thin wall.'

History: 'He was subsequently transferred to the FICU... hypertensive to the 170s and tachycardic to the 130s, with low UOP ranging from ___... UOP dropped to <10cc/hr and has become increasingly difficult to ventilate.'
Prior imaging 2 (CTA Chest): 'No pseudoaneurysm of the splenic artery.'
ensemble.reasoning: This critically ill, intubated patient with necrotizing pancreatitis, known SMV thrombus, and evolving peripancreatic fluid collections requires serial monitoring of vascular and collection status, but transport to CT or MRI is prohibitively risky given hemodynamic instability, ventilator dependence, and open abdomen. A portable (PORT) abdominal ultrasound allows bedside assessment of portal/SMV flow, interval change in peripancreatic fluid, and gallbladder status without moving the patient, directly addressing the gap left by the MRCP finding of 'severe attenuation of the main portal vein... without definite thrombosis' and the progressively organized peripancreatic fluid.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Portal vein / SMV thrombosis progression; peripancreatic fluid collection / pseudoaneurysm development; biliary obstruction recurrence
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether the peripancreatic fluid collections, portal/SMV thrombus, or vascular complications (e.g., splenic artery pseudoaneurysm) have changed since the last cross-sectional imaging, and whether the portal vein compression/attenuation seen on MRCP has progressed to frank thrombosis — all without exposing a critically ill, intubated patient to transport for CT or MRI.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Change in size or character of peripancreatic fluid collections, new or evolving portal vein / SMV thrombus, gallbladder wall changes, or new ascites — assessed at bedside in an ICU-bound patient who cannot safely travel to CT/MRI.

grounding: Prior imaging 2 (CTA Chest): 'There is a small area of thrombus in the SMV seen on series 4B image 130.'
Prior imaging 2 (CTA Chest): 'There is acute edematous ____ with an area of necrosis in the pancreatic neck/proximal body. There is a moderate volume pancreatic ascites.'
Prior imaging 4 (MRCP): 'There is severe attenuation of the main portal vein at the splenic and SMV confluence (series 17, image 26, 24), without definite thrombosis.'
Prior imaging 4 (MRCP): 'The peripancreatic fluid appears slightly more organized since the ___ CT, demonstrating a thin wall.'

History: 'He was subsequently transferred to the FICU... hypertensive to the 170s and tachycardic to the 130s, with low UOP ranging from ___... UOP dropped to <10cc/hr and has become increasingly difficult to ventilate.'
Prior imaging 2 (CTA Chest): 'No pseudoaneurysm of the splenic artery.'
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This critically ill, intubated patient with necrotizing pancreatitis, known SMV thrombus, and evolving peripancreatic fluid collections requires serial monitoring of vascular and collection status, but transport to CT or MRI is prohibitively risky given hemodynamic instability, ventilator dependence, and open abdomen. A portable (PORT) abdominal ultrasound allows bedside assessment of portal/SMV flow, interval change in peripancreatic fluid, and gallbladder status without moving the patient, directly addressing the gap left by the MRCP finding of 'severe attenuation of the main portal vein... without definite thrombosis' and the progressively organized peripancreatic fluid.

vindication.actual_finding: The ultrasound showed a patent main portal vein with hepatopetal flow, no ascites, no gallbladder wall thickening or stones, a diffusely echogenic liver, splenomegaly (16.3 cm), and critically, the pancreas was not visualized due to overlying bowel gas.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The study failed to address the primary clinical questions — peripancreatic fluid collections, portal/SMV thrombus evolution, and vascular complications — because the pancreas and peripancreatic region were entirely obscured by bowel gas, leaving the key information gaps unresolved.

steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS W/O CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Progression of pancreatic necrosis, expansion of peripancreatic fluid collections/walled-off necrosis, SMV thrombus propagation, abdominal compartment syndrome recurrence, or new intra-abdominal complication (e.g., bowel ischemia given SMV thrombus)
ensemble.information_gap: Whether the pancreatic necrosis, peripancreatic fluid collections, or SMV thrombus have changed in extent since the last imaging — particularly whether there is interval progression that could explain the patient's clinical deterioration (worsening ventilation, oliguria, rising plateau pressures) in the setting of an open abdomen
ensemble.expected_finding: Interval increase in peripancreatic fluid/necrosis, new or enlarging walled-off collection, gas within the necrotic region suggesting infected necrosis, or propagation of SMV thrombus; alternatively, stability of these findings to guide conservative management
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has known necrotizing pancreatitis with SMV thrombus, an open abdomen, and is now clinically deteriorating with worsening respiratory mechanics and oliguria, making interval reassessment of intra-abdominal pathology directly actionable; non-contrast CT is appropriate given prior contrast exposure and renal concerns, and avoids contrast nephropathy risk in a patient with oliguria and granular casts.
grounding: CT Chest (prior imaging 2): 'There is acute edematous ____ with an area of necrosis in the pancreatic neck/proximal body. There is a moderate volume pancreatic ascites.'
CT Chest (prior imaging 2): 'There is a small area of thrombus in the SMV seen on series 4B image 130.'
CT Abdomen W/O Contrast (prior imaging 3): 'There is minimal increase in peripancreatic edema compared to prior.'
MRCP (prior imaging 4): 'The peripancreatic fluid appears slightly more organized since the ___ CT, demonstrating a thin wall.'
History: 'his UOP dropped to <10cc/hr and has become increasingly difficult to ventilate, with plateau pressures in the high ___ and peak pressures in the upper ___'
CT Chest (prior imaging 2): 'There is a dehiscent midline abdominal wound created surgically for decompression of abdominal compartment syndrome.'
Labs: 'Granular Casts: 5.0 #/lpf' and 'Protein: 300.0 mg/dL' suggesting renal injury, making contrast avoidance prudent
CT Abdomen W/O Contrast (prior imaging 3): 'There is no evidence of gas within this hypodensity' — absence of gas in necrosis was reassuring previously, now needs reassessment
ensemble.reasoning: This patient with necrotizing pancreatitis, known SMV thrombus, and an open abdomen is now deteriorating with worsening ventilatory mechanics and near-anuric oliguria after 13L of resuscitation. The most pressing question is whether the pancreatic necrosis has progressed, become infected (gas in necrosis), or whether the peripancreatic collections have expanded sufficiently to compromise respiratory or renal function — findings that would directly change management (e.g., percutaneous drainage, surgical intervention). Non-contrast CT is chosen over contrast-enhanced given the oliguria, granular casts, and proteinuria suggesting early acute kidney injury, where additional contrast nephropathy risk is undesirable.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Progression of pancreatic necrosis, expansion of peripancreatic fluid collections/walled-off necrosis, SMV thrombus propagation, abdominal compartment syndrome recurrence, or new intra-abdominal complication (e.g., bowel ischemia given SMV thrombus)
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether the pancreatic necrosis, peripancreatic fluid collections, or SMV thrombus have changed in extent since the last imaging — particularly whether there is interval progression that could explain the patient's clinical deterioration (worsening ventilation, oliguria, rising plateau pressures) in the setting of an open abdomen
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Interval increase in peripancreatic fluid/necrosis, new or enlarging walled-off collection, gas within the necrotic region suggesting infected necrosis, or propagation of SMV thrombus; alternatively, stability of these findings to guide conservative management
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The patient has known necrotizing pancreatitis with SMV thrombus, an open abdomen, and is now clinically deteriorating with worsening respiratory mechanics and oliguria, making interval reassessment of intra-abdominal pathology directly actionable; non-contrast CT is appropriate given prior contrast exposure and renal concerns, and avoids contrast nephropathy risk in a patient with oliguria and granular casts.

CT Abdomen W/O Contrast (prior imaging 3): 'There is minimal increase in peripancreatic edema compared to prior.'
MRCP (prior imaging 4): 'The peripancreatic fluid appears slightly more organized since the ___ CT, demonstrating a thin wall.'

Non-contrast CT is chosen over contrast-enhanced given the oliguria, granular casts, and proteinuria suggesting early acute kidney injury, where additional contrast nephropathy risk is undesirable.

vindication.actual_finding: Multiple peripancreatic and pancreatic acute necrotic collections are present with early organization (head 5.1×3.1 cm, body 3.8×2.1 cm, anterior peripancreatic 10.1×5.9 cm, right lower quadrant 1.9×3.8 cm), with decreased retroperitoneal fluid compared to prior, no gas within collections, and no vascular assessment possible without contrast.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report confirms evolving/organizing necrotic collections consistent with the expected interval change in peripancreatic disease, though the absence of IV contrast limits assessment of SMV thrombus propagation and infected necrosis (no gas identified), partially fulfilling the expected findings while leaving the vascular question unresolved.
